findings.reachability: Any administrator who saves a crafted color value via the plugin settings form; rendered back to any admin who views the settings page
findings.justification: The stored color value is placed into an HTML input attribute via wp_kses_post(stripslashes($value)), which strips disallowed HTML tags but does NOT encode double-quote characters. A value such as `" onmouseover="alert(1)` breaks out of the value attribute and injects an event handler; esc_attr() is required here.

detection_pattern.section_name: Stored XSS: wp_kses_post in HTML attribute
detection_pattern.description: Catches cases where wp_kses_post() (a tag-stripping function) is used to populate an HTML attribute value instead of esc_attr(). wp_kses_post does not encode double-quotes so a stored payload can break out of the attribute and inject event handlers.

findings.reachability: Administrator who controls an attachment URL (e.g. via a crafted attachment record); rendered to any admin viewing the Design settings page
findings.justification: The attachment URL retrieved from wp_get_attachment_image_src() is passed through esc_url() but then injected bare into a CSS style attribute as `style="background-image:url(' . $url . ')"` — without CSS string quotes around the url() argument and without esc_attr() wrapping the entire style value. A URL containing `)` followed by CSS payload can close the url() call and inject arbitrary CSS rules (UI redressing, content injection).

detection_pattern.section_name: CSS Injection: unquoted url() in style attribute
detection_pattern.description: Finds background-image:url() constructions in PHP-generated style attributes where the URL argument is not wrapped in CSS string quotes. Without quotes, a ) character in the URL closes the url() call early and allows injecting subsequent CSS. The anti-pattern checks for a leading quote inside url().

findings.reachability: Latent: no current caller in this file passes attacker-controlled data into val/label; risk materialises if any future or third-party caller passes DB-sourced values
findings.justification: Both $tmp['val'] and $tmp['label'] are interpolated raw into <option> HTML (`value="{$tmp['val']}"` and `>{$tmp['label']} `). The downstream mtnc_wp_kses() call strips disallowed tags but does not re-encode attribute values containing double-quotes, so a val containing `"` breaks the value attribute. esc_attr()/esc_html() should be applied at point of interpolation.

detection_pattern.description: Finds PHP string-interpolated <option value=...> constructions where the value is inserted raw via {$var} rather than esc_attr($var). The anti-pattern confirms esc_attr is applied to the value before interpolation.

findings.reachability: Internal only — called during plugin activation/defaults; no user input reaches the query string
findings.justification: $wpdb->get_results() is called with an unparameterised string literal containing only the WP core $wpdb->posts table name property and a hardcoded LIKE pattern — no user input is interpolated, so there is no practical SQL injection. The issue is a code hygiene/WPCS violation (phpcs:ignore suppresses the warning) that could become exploitable if the query is later modified to include user input.

detection_pattern.description: Finds $wpdb->get_results() calls where the query is passed as a bare double-quoted string (not via $wpdb->prepare()). Files matching this but not having prepare() nearby are candidates for SQL injection if user-controlled values are ever concatenated into those strings.
